evals.name: verbose-quarterly-email
evals.prompt:
Hey, can you /smart-brevity this email I'm about to send to the team? I think it's too long and buries the point.

---

Subject: Quick update on a few things

Hi team,

I hope this finds you all doing well and that you had a great weekend! I wanted to reach out and quickly send over a brief update on a number of items that have been developing over the course of the last few weeks, since I think it's important that everyone is on the same page going into next quarter.

First of all, as many of you know, we've been working very hard on the new pricing model, and I'm pleased to be able to share that after several rounds of iteration and feedback from various stakeholders, we have finally arrived at what we believe is a much improved version that is now actually live in production as of yesterday afternoon. Early signs are looking quite positive, with conversion rates appearing to be up by approximately 18% compared to last month, although it is admittedly still early days.

Secondly, on the hiring side of things, we've successfully managed to close out two senior engineering hires this week, which is great news and brings our total Q1 hires up to seven, putting us comfortably ahead of the plan that we had originally laid out at the start of the year. Both new joiners are going to be starting next month and we'll be sending around proper intros closer to the time.

Lastly, I just wanted to also flag that there is going to be a company-wide offsite happening in approximately six weeks from now, more details to follow, but please do block off the week of June 9th in your calendars as a heads up.

Thanks so much, and please do let me know if there are any questions or anything I can help clarify.

Best,
Jan
evals.expected_output: An HTML diff opens in the browser. The 'after' is ~50-70% shorter, leads with the news (pricing live, conversion +18%), uses bullets/bolded headlines, has explicit 'Why it matters' callouts, drops the 'hope this finds you well' / 'wanted to reach out' / 'as many of you know' warm-ups. Blocks are numbered [1], [2], [3] (flat, no nested sections) since there are no formal headers.

evals.name: long-jargon-memo
evals.prompt:
/smart-brevity — please rewrite this internal memo. It's making my eyes glaze over.

---

# Q1 Operational Review

## Background

Over the course of the past quarter, our organization has been engaged in a number of strategic initiatives across multiple functional areas, with the primary goal of driving operational efficiency improvements and aligning cross-functional teams around our shared OKRs for the fiscal year. This memo serves as a synthesis of the key developments and is intended to provide leadership with a holistic view of progress to date.

## Customer Experience

We have made considerable investments in our customer-facing infrastructure, including but not limited to a comprehensive overhaul of our support ticketing workflow, the deployment of an enhanced self-service knowledge base, and the rollout of a new in-product feedback mechanism. As a direct result of these initiatives, we have observed a meaningful reduction in average time-to-resolution metrics, with the current quarter-end figure standing at approximately 4.2 hours, which represents a 31% improvement relative to the prior quarter's baseline.

## Product Velocity

From a product development perspective, the engineering organization has been operating at an elevated cadence throughout Q1, with a total of 47 features shipped to production and a corresponding incident rate that has remained well within our service-level objective thresholds. We attribute this success to the operational discipline introduced through the new release-train methodology that was adopted earlier this year.

## Looking Ahead

In closing, we believe that the momentum we have built across these various workstreams positions the organization favorably for Q2, and we will continue to provide regular updates as we operationalize the next phase of our roadmap.
evals.expected_output: An HTML diff opens in the browser using nested section numbering ([1.1], [2.1], [3.1], [4.1]). Each section opens with its conclusion as a bolded headline (e.g. 'Support resolution times dropped 31% to 4.2hr.'). Jargon ('operational efficiency', 'service-level objective thresholds', 'operationalize') is replaced with plain words. Section 'Why it matters' callouts are present where stakes are clear. Final word count is at least 50% lower than the source.

evals.name: slack-rambling
evals.prompt:
Can you /smart-brevity this Slack message I'm about to post in #engineering? It's way too long for chat.

---

hey everyone, so i wanted to just kind of mention something that came up earlier today in the discussion with the platform team that i think is worth being aware of going forward. basically what we found is that there's been an intermittent issue with the staging environment where occasionally requests to the auth service have been timing out, and after some investigation it looks like the root cause is actually related to the connection pool configuration that we put in place a few sprints back when we did that refactor. so the proposed fix is to bump the pool size from 20 to 50 and also add some better retry logic on the client side. we're planning to ship that tomorrow morning. anyway just wanted to flag this in case anyone has been seeing weirdness in staging.
evals.expected_output: An HTML diff opens with a single block (or 2-3 short blocks) since this is a Slack message. The rewrite is dramatically shorter — under 50 words. It leads with the action ('Fixing intermittent staging auth timeouts tomorrow') and lists the cause and fix as bullets. No 'Why it matters' callout (rules say skip for chat). No warm-up like 'hey everyone' or 'i wanted to just kind of mention'.
